chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing infections in severe acute malnutrition (SAM) focus on early antibiotic use, reassessment timelines, and syndrome-specific treatment durations to prevent undertreatment or overuse.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of early antibiotic initiation in complicated SAM, even without typical infection signs.
Learn to use physiologic danger signs as infection triggers and reassess therapy at defined intervals.
Apply syndrome-specific antibiotic durations and escalate care for non-responding cases.
key_facts: In complicated SAM, assume bacterial infection even without typical signs like fever.
Reassess therapy at 48–72 hours and again at day 5 to adjust treatment based on clinical progress.
Minimum antibiotic durations: sepsis (≥7 days), UTI (7–10 days), meningitis (14–21 days), osteomyelitis (~4 weeks).
Culture samples should be collected before antibiotics if feasible, but treatment must not be delayed in unstable children.
Non-improvement by day 5 mandates reassessment for resistant organisms, misdiagnosis, or comorbidities like TB or HIV.
reference_quote: In complicated SAM, assume bacterial infection even when typical signs are absent. Antibiotic prescribing should be standardized and time-linked to reassessment. (Source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Operational guidelines on facility-based management of children with severe acute malnutrition, 2011)
